parsed.goal: Deep philosophical mastery and first-principles understanding of Advaita Vedanta

modules.title: First Principles: The Architecture of Non-Dualism
modules.description: Before opening the Gita, we must build the Advaita framework from scratch. This module introduces Shankaracharya's foundational epistemology, the concept of Adhyasa (superimposition), and the rigorous qualifications required of the learner.

plan.goal: Define Advaita Vedanta from first principles, explain the mechanics of Adhyasa, and evaluate personal readiness using Sadhana Chatushtaya.
concepts: Adhyasa (Superimposition of Self and non-Self)
Avidya (Ignorance) vs. Vidya (Knowledge)
Sadhana Chatushtaya (The Four-fold Qualifications)
Viveka (Discrimination)
Vairagya (Dispassion)
Brahman vs. Atman
The Three Levels of Truth (Paramarthika, Vyavaharika, Pratibhasika)
The Snake and the Rope Analogy

modules.title: Shankaracharya's Upodghata (Introduction)
modules.description: An exhaustive analysis of Shankaracharya's preamble to the Gita commentary. This sets the theological and philosophical thesis for the entire 18-chapter journey.

plan.goal: Analyze Shankaracharya's preamble, strictly distinguishing between Pravritti Dharma (action) and Nivritti Dharma (renunciation), and defend his thesis that Jnana is the sole means to Moksha.
concepts: Pravritti Dharma (The Path of Action/Prosperity)
Nivritti Dharma (The Path of Renunciation/Liberation)
The role of Narayana/Vasudeva as the Supreme Creator
The purpose of the Bhagavad Gita according to Shankara
Jnana (Knowledge) vs. Karma (Action) dichotomy
Moksha as realization, not an attainment

modules.title: The Anatomy of Samsara: Arjuna's Delusion
modules.description: Examining Chapter 1 not merely as a historical narrative, but as Shankaracharya views it: a precise clinical presentation of Avidya (ignorance), attachment, and grief.

plan.goal: Deconstruct Arjuna's grief as the archetypal manifestation of Avidya and Adhyasa, proving that ethical dilemmas born of worldly attachment cannot be solved by empirical logic.
concepts: Shoka (Grief) and Moha (Delusion)
The psychological mechanism of attachment
The breakdown of empirical Dharma under the weight of Avidya
Arjuna as the archetype of the bound soul (Jiva)
The necessity of a Guru for spiritual awakening
sourceRefs: First Discourse: The Despondency of Arjuna (pp. 7-18)

modules.title: The Metaphysical Core: Sankhya-Yoga & The Buddhist Dialectic
modules.description: The philosophical heart of the Gita. Here we do a deep dive into verse-by-verse commentary to establish the absolute reality of the Atman, contrasting Shankara's unchanging Witness with Buddhist Sunyata and Momentariness.

plan.goal: Master the absolute distinction between the unchanging Self (Atman) and the ephemeral non-Self, and critically defend Advaita's 'Unchanging Witness' against the Buddhist theories of Sunyata and Momentariness.
concepts: Sat (The Real) vs. Asat (The Unreal)
The nature of the Atman (Unborn, Eternal, Unchanging)
Critique of Buddhist Kshanikavada (Momentariness)
Critique of Buddhist Sunyavada (Emptiness/Nihilism)
The necessity of a Sakshi (Witnessing Consciousness)
Sthitaprajna (The man of steady wisdom)
The mechanics of sensory withdrawal (Kurma/Tortoise analogy)

modules.title: The Mechanics of Purification: Karma and Jnana
modules.description: Shankaracharya fiercely opposes the idea that action (Karma) directly leads to liberation. This module explores how Karma is reframed strictly as a preparatory tool (Sadhana) to purify the mind for Jnana.

plan.goal: Explain Shankaracharya's subordination of Karma to Jnana, demonstrating how selfless action purifies the mind (Chitta Shuddhi) but cannot destroy existential ignorance.
concepts: Karma-Yoga as preparatory purification (Chitta Shuddhi)
The impossibility of Karma-Jnana Samuccaya (combining action and knowledge)
Action in Inaction and Inaction in Action
Yajna (Sacrifice) as a cosmic principle
Loka-Sangraha (Maintenance of the world)
The descent of the Avatar (Avatara Rahasya)

modules.title: Renunciation and the Architecture of Meditation
modules.description: Moving from action to stillness. We analyze Shankara's definitions of true Sannyasa (monastic renunciation) and the precise, step-by-step psychological framework for Dhyana (meditation).

plan.goal: Reconcile outer action with inner renunciation, and apply the Advaitic method of Dhyana to achieve absolute stillness of the mind.
concepts: True Sannyasa vs. physical renunciation
The equivalence of Sankhya and Yoga in outcome
The mechanics of Dhyana (Meditation)
Overcoming Vikshepa (mental agitation)
The lamp in a windless place analogy
The fate of the fallen Yogi (Yoga-bhrashta)

modules.title: The Macrocosm: Saguna Brahman and Maya
modules.description: Shifting to theology and cosmology. How does the non-dual Brahman appear as a diverse universe? We explore the concept of Maya and the distinction between God with attributes (Ishvara) and the Absolute.

plan.goal: Articulate the relationship between Saguna Brahman (Ishvara), Nirguna Brahman, and the mechanism of Maya that projects the cosmos.
concepts: Apara Prakriti (Lower Nature) vs. Para Prakriti (Higher Nature)
Maya as the indefinable, projecting power of Brahman
Saguna Brahman (Ishvara) vs. Nirguna Brahman
The four types of devotees (Bhakta)
The mechanics of death and the final thought (Anta-kala)
The paths of light and dark (Devayana and Pitriyana)
prerequisites: The Three Levels of Truth (Paramarthika, Vyavaharika, Pratibhasika)

modules.title: Sovereign Knowledge and the Universal Form
modules.description: Examining Shankara's integration of high devotion (Bhakti) within a strict non-dual framework, culminating in the terrifying and magnificent vision of the Vishvarupa (Universal Form) as an empirical projection.

plan.goal: Interpret Krishna's divine manifestations (Vibhutis) and the Universal Form (Vishvarupa) as Vyavaharika (empirical) realities superimposed on the absolute Brahman.
concepts: Raja-Vidya (The Sovereign Knowledge)
Advaitic Bhakti: Seeing the Self in all
Vibhuti: The divine glories manifesting in the empirical world
Vishvarupa Darshana: The Universal Form
Time (Kala) as the ultimate destroyer in the empirical realm
The distinction between seeing the Form and knowing the Essence
sourceRefs: Ninth Discourse: Sovereign Wisdom and Secret (pp. 238-258)
prerequisites: Maya as the indefinable, projecting power of Brahman

modules.title: The Knower of the Field: High Metaphysics Reborn
modules.description: After the devotional chapters, Shankara returns to pure, rigorous Advaita metaphysics. We analyze Chapter 13 verse-by-verse to permanently separate the Observer from the Observed.

plan.goal: Distinguish between the Field (Kshetra) and the Knower of the Field (Kshetrajna), fully equating the individual Knower with the Supreme Brahman.
concepts: Kshetra (The Field / Object / Matter)
Kshetrajna (The Knower of the Field / Subject / Spirit)
The absolute identity of Kshetrajna and Brahman
Jneya (That which is to be known)
Prakriti and Purusha in the Advaitic context
The Sun and its Reflections analogy
prerequisites: The necessity of a Sakshi (Witnessing Consciousness)

modules.title: The Matrix of Prakriti and the Supreme Spirit
modules.description: Deconstructing the material world into its three constituent Gunas, and analyzing the famous Ashvattha Tree analogy to map the escape route from Samsara to the Purushottama.

plan.goal: Deconstruct the three Gunas of Prakriti, analyze the Ashvattha tree analogy as the ultimate map of Samsara, and identify the Purushottama (Supreme Spirit) transcending it.
concepts: The Three Gunas: Sattva, Rajas, Tamas
Gunatita (Transcending the Gunas)
The upside-down Ashvattha Tree analogy of Samsara
Cutting the tree with the axe of Vairagya (Detachment)
Kshara (The Perishable), Akshara (The Imperishable), and Purushottama (The Supreme)

modules.title: Empirical Ethics and the Anatomy of Faith
modules.description: Before the final conclusion, Shankara maps the Vyavaharika (empirical) landscape of human behavior, distinguishing between liberating and binding psychological traits.

plan.goal: Evaluate the role of empirical ethics (Daivi vs Asuri Sampad) and the three-fold faith (Shraddha) in either binding the soul or preparing it for self-knowledge.
concepts: Daivi Sampad (Divine Heritage) vs. Asuri Sampad (Demoniac Heritage)
The three gates to hell: Lust, Anger, Greed
Shraddha (Faith) and its three-fold nature based on Gunas
The three types of food, sacrifice, austerity, and charity
Om Tat Sat: The supreme mantra of purification
prerequisites: The Three Gunas: Sattva, Rajas, Tamas

modules.title: The Grand Synthesis: Absolute Liberation
modules.description: The capstone module. An exhaustive study of Chapter 18, where Shankaracharya weaves all previous arguments together to deliver his final, unyielding verdict on the sole supremacy of Jnana.

plan.goal: Synthesize the entire 18-chapter commentary to defend Shankaracharya's final thesis: Knowledge alone (Jnana), completely divorced from Action (Karma), is the direct and only cause of Moksha.
concepts: Sannyasa vs. Tyaga in final analysis
The five factors of action (refuting agency of the Self)
The three-fold nature of knowledge, action, and the doer
The culmination of Karma-Yoga into Jnana-Nishta
Sarva-Dharman Parityajya: The ultimate relinquishment of all empirical duties
Moksha as the removal of Adhyasa, revealing the ever-present Brahman
prerequisites: The impossibility of Karma-Jnana Samuccaya (combining action and knowledge)
The absolute identity of Kshetrajna and Brahman

Shankaracharya's Upodghata (Introduction)
